coredata.dc:title: Luminescence responses of CePO4: Tb monospheres toward vitamin C and permanganate
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xuejiao
Wang, Zhihao
Huang, Sai
Shi, Xiaofei
Li, Ji-Guang
coredata.dc:description: (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monospheres (˜1.39 ± 0.11 μm) of negligible aggregation and high geometric uniformity were synthesized via microwave assisted homogeneous precipitation, whose luminescence responses toward MnO4 − oxidant and vitamin C reductant were studied in detail. Through exciting Ce3+ with 275 nm UV light, strong and broad UV emission centered at ˜350 nm (FWHM ˜40 nm) and sharp green emission at ˜544 nm (FWHM ˜10 nm) were obtained, and efficient Ce3+ → Tb3+ energy transfer, with an efficiency of ˜65%, was observed. Interacting the monospheres with MnO4 − (0–50 μM) effectively quenched the luminescence via Ce3+ oxidation, and treating the luminescence-quenched monospheres with VC (0–250 μM) sufficiently recovered the luminescence via Ce4+ reduction. Numerical expressions were also established to correlate the emission intensity with VC and MnO4 − concentrations. Besides, the colloidal monospheres exhibited the favorable response time of ˜18 and 50 s for 25 μM of MnO4 − and 150 μM of VC, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4
Colloidal monosphere
Luminescence switching
Vitamin C
MnO4 −

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescence responses of the monospheres toward MnO4 − and VC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM (a) and TEM (b) morphologies, HR-TEM lattice imaging (c) and SAED pattern (d) of the CePO4 monospheres. (e) and (f) are the FE-SEM morphology of the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monospheres and a photograph showing the appearance of the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 colloidal suspension after still standing for 2 h.
N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K for the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monospheres.
XRD patterns of CePO4 (a) and (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 (b). The standard diffractions of hexagonal structured CePO4 (JCPDS No. 35-0614) are included as bars for comparison.

Excitation (a, c) and emission (b, d) spectra of the CePO4 (a, b) and TbPO4 (c, d) monospheres. The excitation and emission wavelengths used for the measurements and assignments of the electronic transitions are included in the figure.

Excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monospheres. The excitation and emission wavelengths used for the measurements are indicated in the figure, and the emission spectrum of TbPO4, taken under the same measurement condition of (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4, is included in part (b) for comparison.

Luminescence quenching of the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 colloidal suspension, as a function of the concentration of added KMnO4 (a) and luminescence recovery of the already quenched (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 colloidal solution, as a function of the concentration of added VC (b).
Single-exponential (a) and linear (b) fitting of the 544 and 350 nm luminescence intensities with the concentrations of KMnO4 (a) and VC (b) for the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 colloidal suspension.

Time-course quenching of the 544 nm Tb3+ emission by 25 μM KMnO4 (red square) and subsequent recovery of the emission by 150 μM VC (green square). The inserts are enlarged views of the initial phases of MnO4 − (left) and VC (right) addition (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Luminescence responses of CePO4: Tb monospheres toward vitamin C and permanganate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Energy, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121007, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Functional Materials, National Institute for Materials Science, Tsukuba, Ibaraki, 305-0044, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials (Ministry of Education), Northeastern University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Ceramics and Powder Metallurgy, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Ceramics and Powder Metallurgy
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of New Energy, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121007, China; Research Center for Functional Materials, National Institute for Materials Science, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-0044, Japan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jinzhou, Liaoning 121007, China; Research Center for Functional Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monospheres (˜1.39 ± 0.11 μm) of negligible aggregation and high geometric uniformity were synthesized via microwave assisted homogeneous precipitation, whose luminescence responses toward MnO4 − oxidant and vitamin C reductant were studied in detail. Through exciting Ce3+ with 275 nm UV light, strong and broad UV emission centered at ˜350 nm (FWHM ˜40 nm) and sharp green emission at ˜544 nm (FWHM ˜10 nm) were obtained, and efficient Ce3+ → Tb3+ energy transfer, with an efficiency of ˜65%, was observed. Interacting the monospheres with MnO4 − (0–50 μM) effectively quenched the luminescence via Ce3+ oxidation, and treating the luminescence-quenched monospheres with VC (0–250 μM) sufficiently recovered the luminescence via Ce4+ reduction. Numerical expressions were also established to correlate the emission intensity with VC and MnO4 − concentrations. Besides, the colloidal monospheres exhibited the favorable response time of ˜18 and 50 s for 25 μM of MnO4 − and 150 μM of VC, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare-earth (RE) doped inorganic compounds are being considered as an important family of fluorescent probes, since they generally have the advantages of low toxicity, sharp emission, and high resistance to photo bleaching and blinking. The systems that have been established so far typically include one-dimensional (1D) CePO4:Tb and LaPO4:Ce/Tb nanocrystallites [1–6], 2D plates and films of (Y0.9Ce0.1)2(OH)5Cl·1.5H2O [7], Ce(OH)2Cl [8], Ce(OH)2Cl:Tb [8] and Ce2(OH)4SO4·2H2O [9] layered hydroxides (LHs), and 0D LaF3:Ce/Tb nanoparticles [10]. The LHs are of interest because of their rich interlayer chemistry for anion exchange, pollutant trapping and chemical sensing [11–19]. They, however, show high sensitivity to solution pH and readily undergo dissolution in a mildly acid solution (pH ˜5) or composition change with increasing pH [20], which limits their practical application. In this regard, CePO4:Tb and LaPO4:Ce/Tb are significantly more advantageous as fluorescent probes because of their high resistance to pH variation, negligible water solubility (K sp ˜10−25-10-27), and low toxicity. REPO4 is well known to strongly undergo anisotropic crystallization to form the hexagonal phase (h-REPO4; P6222 space group) for the larger RE3+ of La-Tb in the lanthanide family [21,22], which accounts for why the solution-synthesized CePO4:Tb products are always 1D structured [1–5,23,24]. Our literature survey also showed that geometry uniformity control of the 1D crystallites is rather difficult without employing surfactant, while the use of surfactant inevitably causes surface contamination and poor fluorescence and sensing capability. It is well established that the physical/chemical property of an individual particle/crystallite is dimension-governed. The differently sized 1D crystallites of CePO4:Tb would thus exhibit differing sensitivities toward analyte in practice, since detection largely involves surface-sensitive redox reactions that govern luminescence.

Vitamin C (VC, C8H8O6) is a vital nutrient that plays multifunctional roles in human life, and is being widely used in various chemical, biological, pharmaceutical and nutritional systems [1,25,26]. Permanganate (MnO4 −), on the other hand, is one of the most important and commonly used strong oxidants, which serves as an antiseptic and disinfectant for fish disease treatment, water purification, and medical sanitization [27]. Excess MnO4 −, however, can cause severe damage to human health and the environment [28–30]. An effective and sensitive approach for the fast and precise detection of VC and MnO4 − is hence highly desired in medical assay and pollutant evaluation [1]. For such purposes, a number of analytical techniques have been developed up to date, mainly including liquid/ion chromatography, atomic absorption spectrometry, spectrophotometry, colorimetry, titrimetry and electrochemical analysis [31–45]. Some of the techniques, however, have the drawbacks of low sensitivity, complicated operation procedure, time consumption, and high cost.

We synthesized in this work (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 spheres of excellent geometric uniformity and negligible aggregation (monospheres) and their luminescence responses toward VC and MnO4 − were studied in detail. The spherical particle shape may minimize surface scattering of the excitation/emission light to produce a better luminescence [46], while the excellent size uniformity and negligible aggregation may allow synchronized responses among the spheres. With these, potential application in analyte detection for a better precision and reliability may be possible. To the best of our knowledge, a (Ce,Tb)PO4 sensing system comprising monospheres for either VC or MnO4 − analyte has not been reported before.

The rare-earth (RE) sources are 99.96% pure Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 99.99% pure Tb4O7 (Huizhou Ruier Rare-Chem. Hi-Tech. Co. Ltd., Huizhou China). The other reagents of nitric acid (HNO3, 63 wt%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 wt%), ammonium sulfate (NH4)2SO4, 99% pure), urea (CO(NH2)2, 99% pure), KMnO4 (>99% pure) and vitamin C (VC, >99% pure) were purchased from Shenyang Chemical Reagent Factory (Shenyang, China). Milli-Q filtered water (resistivity ˜18 MΩ·cm) was used throughout the experiments. The nitrate solution of RE3+ (0.2 mol/L) was prepared by directly dissolving the Ce(NO3)3·6H2O particles in water while that of Tb3+ was obtained by dissolving the Tb4O7 powder with a proper amount of nitric acid, followed by evaporation to dryness to remove any superfluous HNO3 and dilution with water.

The CePO4:Tb monospheres were synthesized via microwave assisted homogeneous precipitation in the presence of SO4 2− (from (NH4)2SO4) coagulant [46,47]. In a typical synthesis, 500 ml of an aqueous solution (pH ˜1) containing 50 mmol of H3PO4, 2.5 mmol of (NH4)2SO4, 0.25 mol of CO(NH2)2 and 4 mmol of RE3+ (RE = Ce0.90Tb0.10) was subjected to microwave irradiation (700 W) for four minutes. The resultant (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 monospheres were collected via centrifugation, followed by water washing via ultrasonication three times, rinsing with absolute ethanol once, and then drying in an air oven at 70 °C for 24 h. For comparison and a better understanding of the luminescence behaviors of (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4, CePO4 and TbPO4 monospheres were also synthesized by the above protocol.

Phase identification was performed via X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Model Smart Lab3, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) under 40 kV/40 mA, using nickel-filtered Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) and a scanning speed of 4°/2θ per minute. Morphology and microstructure of the product were analyzed via field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Model JSM-7011 F, JEOL, Tokyo) under 10 kV and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Model JEM-2000FX, JEOL) under 200 kV. Adsorption/desorption isotherms of the spheres were measured via the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method in liquid nitrogen at 77 K, using a Quantachrome autosorb-1 analyzer (Quantachrome Instruments, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Model Nicolet iS5, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was performed by the standard KBr pellet method. Photoluminescence excitation and emission (PL/PLE) spectra of the monospheres were taken at room temperature using an FP-8600 fluorospectrophotometer (JASCO, Tokyo) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp for excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence responses of the monospheres toward MnO4 − and VC
ce:section.ce:para: 4 mg of the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monospheres were ultrasonically dispersed in water to produce a colloidal suspension of 2 mL. The luminescence responses of (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 upon reacting with KMnO4 oxidant and VC reductant in the suspension were examined by monitoring the photoluminescence spectra. In the oxidation process (luminescence quenching), a KMnO4 solution (10−3 mol/L) was sequentially added into the colloidal suspension in the volumes of 5, 5, 10, 10, 20, 25 and 25 μL, respectively, followed by 3 s of ultrasonication for homogenization each time. The total volume of the added KMnO4 solution is 100 μL (0.1 mL, totally 0.1 μmol of KMnO4). In each case, PL/PLE spectra were recorded for the colloidal suspension after 9 min of KMnO4 addition. In the reduction process (luminescence recovery), a VC solution (10-2 mol/L) was stepwise added into the already luminescence quenched (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 suspension in the volumes of 10, 5, 5, 5, 5, 10 and 10 μL, respectively, followed by the ultrasonication and luminescence analysis described above. The total volume of the added VC solution is 50 μL (0.05 mL, totally 0.5 μmol of VC).

In a separate set of experiments, time-dependent PL spectra were taken for the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 colloidal suspension to evaluate the response time, as follows. For luminescence quenching, 50 μL of the KMnO4 solution was added into the colloidal suspension (MnO4 − concentration: 25 μM) and, after 3 s of ultrasonication, PL spectra were taken in the spectral range of 520–570 nm at the time intervals of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50, 70, 110, 170, 230, 290, 350, 410 and 530 s, respectively. For luminescence recovery, 30 μL of the VC solution was added into the above luminescence quenched suspension (VC concentration: 150 μM), followed by 3 s of ultrasonication and PL measurements at the time intervals of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 60, 80, 120, 180, 240, 300, 360 and 480 s, respectively.

Fig. 1 a shows the FE-SEM morphology of the CePO4 product. It can be clearly seen that it is solely composed of uniformly sized spheres, whose average diameter was determined to be ˜1.36 ± 0.17 μm from the FE-SEM image. TEM observation (Fig. 1b) found that the spheres are well dispersed from each other and have solid interiors. The slightly rough periphery of each sphere conforms to sphere formation via an aggregation mechanism. That is, the negatively charged SO4 2− anions in the reaction system efficiently coagulate the positively charged nuclei/primary crystallites into spheres via charge neutralization [47]. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) analysis of the periphery region showed that the sphere contains primary crystallites of ˜15 nm (Fig. 1c), further validating the coagulation mechanism. The well resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of ˜3.0 Å conform well to the (200) crystal plane of hexagonal-structured CePO4 (3.010 Å in JCPDS Card No. 35-0614) and confirmed the crystalline nature of the sphere. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED, Fig. 1d) yielded spot rings, indicating polycrystallinity of the sphere, and those from the {110} and {211} planes can be clearly identified. Fig. 1e shows the FE-SEM morphology of the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 product, where it is evident that replacing 10 at% of Ce3+ with Tb3+ did not bring about any appreciable change to the characteristics of the spheres, including morphology uniformity, dispersion and average sphere diameter (˜1.39 ± 0.11 μm). This is understandable from the same crystallization habits of CePO4 and TbPO4, as mentioned earlier, and the similar solution chemistry of Ce3+ and Tb3+ arising from the similar ionic radii (0.1143 nm for Ce3+ and 0.1040 nm for Tb3+; CN = 8) [48]. The suspension (2 mL) obtained by ultrasonically dispersing 4 mg of the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 spheres in water shows a uniform appearance and is stable for at least 2 h (Fig. 1f). It can also be assayed with the average diameter (˜1.39 μm) and theoretical density (4.113 g/cm3) of (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 that the colloidal suspension would contain ˜6.8 × 108 individual spheres. Fig. 2 shows the adsorption/desorption isotherms of the dried (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monosheres. The curves are featured by a type-III behavior, which indicates a weak interaction of the spheres with N2. The adsorption and desorption curves almost overlap with each other, implying a nonporous nature of the monospheres. The specific surface area (SBET) of the powder was found to be ˜4.2 m2/g, from which the average sphere diameter (DBET) was estimated with the theoretical density of (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 (dth, 4.113 g/cm3) to be ˜347 nm according to the equation [49]
(1)
D
B E T
=
6000
d
t h
×
S
B E T

The DBET value is substantially smaller than that (˜1.39 ± 0.11 μm) assayed via FE-SEM, primarily due to the rough surfaces of the monosheres since Eq. (1) assumes equivalent solid spheres of smooth surface.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the CePO4 and (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 products. It can be seen that all the diffraction peaks can be well indexed with the hexagonal structured CePO4 phase (JCPDS No. 35-0614) in each case. The diffraction peaks of (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 slightly drifted to larger angles when compared with those of the CePO4 of this work and that in the standard diffraction file (No. 35-0614), which confirmed the substitution of larger Ce3+ by smaller Tb3+ and the formation of solid solution. Broadening analysis of the XRD patterns with the Scherrer formula yielded average crystallite sizes of ˜15.8 nm for both the powders, which further indicates the significantly polycrystalline nature of the spheres and the trial effect of Tb3+ substitution on nucleation/crystallite growth.

FT-IR analysis was performed to further investigate the functional groups built in the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 spheres, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The absorption bands resolved in the ˜450-1300 cm−1 spectral region are attributed to the PO4 3- ligand, with the ν 3 (PO antisymmetric stretching mode) and ν 4 (PO bending mode) vibrations doubly split [21,50]. The ν 1 mode (PO symmetric stretching) of PO4 3- was reported to occur between ν 3 and ν 4 [21,50], as also observed at ˜900 cm−1 in this work. The absorptions at ˜3480 and 1628 cm−1 can be assigned to the OH stretching vibrations (ν 1 and ν 3) and HOH bending mode (ν 2) of water molecules, respectively [21,50]. The FT-IR analysis did not reveal the presence of any alien species, which confirmed that the monospheres are chemically pure and surface clean. This is essentially important for potential analyte detection, where surface interactions are largely involved.

The excitation and emission behaviors of CePO4 (Fig. 1a) and TbPO4 (Fig. S1, average diameter ˜1.38 ± 0.09 μm) dry powders were first analyzed in Fig. 5 to lay a basis for a better understanding of (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 luminescence. The CePO4 spheres show broad-band excitation in the 200–330 nm spectral region (maximum at ˜275 nm, Fig. 5a) upon monitoring the center wavelength (˜350 nm) of Ce3+ emission (Fig. 5b). The excitation bands are associated with electronic transitions from the 2F5/2 level of 4f ground state to the crystal-field split 5d energy levels [30,51]. Under 275 nm excitation, the CePO4 monospheres exhibited UV emission in the ˜300-420 nm spectral region, which arises from the parity-allowed 5d1 → 2F5/2,7/2 transitions (Fig. 5b). The PLE spectrum of TbPO4 (Fig. 5c), obtained by monitoring the 544 nm green emission of Tb3+, is overwhelmingly dominated by the spin-allowed (low-spin, LS) 4f8 → 4f75d1 inter-configurational transition of Tb3+ at ˜214 nm. The peak at ˜257 nm is attributed to the spin-forbidden (high-spin, HS) 4f8 → 4f75d1 transition and the other weak bands in the longer wavelength region (˜280-500 nm) are owing to intra-4f8 electronic transitions of Tb3+ [52]. The PL spectrum (Fig. 5d) taken for the TbPO4 monospheres under 214 nm excitation consists of sharp peaks in the ˜450-700 nm region, which correspond to transitions from the 5D4 excited state to the 7FJ (J = 6-3) ground states of Tb3+ [52], with the 5D4 → 7F5 green emission at ˜544 nm being the most prominent.

Fig. 6 shows the PLE and PL spectra of the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 dry powder. Comparing Fig. 6a with Fig. 5a,c clearly revealed that the excitation spectrum of (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 is dominated by the 4f → 5d transition of Ce3+, instead of the 214 nm LS transition of Tb3+, although the spectrum was taken by monitoring the 544 nm Tb3+ emission (Fig. 6a). The negligibly weak excitation peaks in the ˜330-500 nm region can be assigned to Tb3+ transitions within the 4f shell according to Fig. 5c. The above observations imply the presence of efficient Ce3+ → Tb3+ energy transfer and that the Tb3+ emission can be turned on and off via the redox of host Ce3+ ions. Under 275 nm UV excitation (excitation of Ce3+), the (Ce0.9Tb0.1)PO4 monospheres showed an intensity ˜63 times that of the TbPO4 spheres for the 544 nm Tb3+ emission (Fig. 6b), which further proved the occurrence of efficient Ce3+ → Tb3+ energy transfer. Accordingly, the UV emission of Ce3+ in the ˜300-420 nm region was remarkably weaker than that of CePO4 (Fig. 5b). The efficiency (η) of energy transfer can be estimated with the equation η = 1-(I/I 0), where I and I 0 denote the integrated intensities of Ce3+ emission in the presence (Fig. 6b) and absence (Fig. 5b) of Tb3+ ions, and the value was found to be ˜65%.

Fig. 7 shows the luminescence responses of the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 colloidal suspension toward KMnO4 (Fig. 7a) and VC (Fig. 7b). In the absence of MnO4 − (0 μM, Fig. 7a) and under 275 nm excitation, the suspension exhibited a strong and broad luminescence band, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of ˜40 nm, in the ˜300-420 nm UV region (centered at ˜350 nm) for the 5d 1 → 2F5/2,7/2 transition of Ce3+ and sharp peaks in the ˜470-640 nm visible-light region for the 5D4 → 7FJ (J = 6-3) transitions of Tb3+ [17,15]. The 544 nm green emission of Tb3+ was found to have an FWHM value of ˜10 nm. Again, the Tb3+ luminescence was resulted from Ce3+ → Tb3+ energy transfer, since the excitation wavelength corresponds to the absorption of Ce3+ as discussed earlier. Comparing Fig. 7a with Fig. 6b showed that the I 544/I 350 intensity ratio of Tb3+ and Ce3+ luminescence is substantially smaller for the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 suspension (I 544/I 350 = 1.1; 0 μM of MnO4 −) than that for the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 dry powder (I 544/I 350 = 2.9), which may imply that Tb3+ is more affected by water molecules. Both the 544 nm Tb3+ and 350 nm Ce3+ emissions were successively weakened with increasing KMnO4 addition (Fig. 7a), and they retained ˜58% and 54% of their initial values at 5 μM of KMnO4, respectively. It can also be seen that both the luminescence was almost totally quenched at 50 μM of KMnO4 (Fig. 7a). The luminescence quenching is owing to the redox reaction 3Ce3+ + MnO4 − + 2H2O → 3Ce4+ + MnO2 + 4OH−. That is, oxidation of luminescent Ce3+ into non-luminescent Ce4+ by MnO4 − and the cut-off of Ce3+ → Tb3+ energy transfer [2]. Fig. 8 a presents the emission intensities of Ce3+ (350 nm) and Tb3+ (544 nm) as a function of the KMnO4 concentration, where very similar responses were observed for both the luminescence. It is very encouraging to find that the data can be satisfactorily correlated via the single exponentials of I = 0.02061 + 0.93435exp(-0.11073C) for Ce3+ (goodness factor of fitting: R = 0.9855) and I = 0.0225 + 0.94115exp(-0.09377C) for Tb3+ (R = 0.99162), where I and C denote the emission intensity and KMnO4 concentration, respectively. The actual concentration of MnO4 − can thus be derived from either of the above equations in practice, which makes it possible to detect MnO4 − in a dual-window mode with the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 monospheres.

Addition of VC to the colloidal suspension quenched by 50 μM of MnO4 − can effectively recover (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 luminescence. Successively stronger Tb3+ (544 nm) and Ce3+ (350 nm) emissions were observed with increasing VC concentration, which reached ˜60 and 56% of their initial intensities at 150 μM of VC, respectively. It can also be seen by comparing Fig. 7a and b that 250 μM of VC has basically restored the quenched luminescence to the original levels for both Ce3+ and Tb3+. VC addition would induce the redox reaction 2Ce4+ + C6H8O6 → 2Ce3+ + C6H6O6 + 2H+, and, therefore, turned on the luminescence of both Ce3+ and Tb3+. Fig. 8b plots the emission intensity against VC concentration, from which the good linear relations of I = -0.13 + 0.00459C and I = -0.23 + 0.005C can be derived for the 544 and 350 nm luminescence of Tb3+ (goodness factor of fitting: R = 0.99527) and Ce3+ (R = 0.99562), respectively. The results also suggest that the emissions of Ce3+ and Tb3+ exhibit similar responses toward VC.

The above satisfactory results of data fitting would be benefited from the excellent morphology uniformity of the monospheres, which allows for synchronized responses among the spheres. The Ce4+/Ce3+ and Tb4+/Tb3+ pairs have their respective standard reduction potentials (E°) of 1.73 and 3.05 V [53], which implies that Ce3+ is much more redoxable than Tb3+ under the same condition, as also reported by previous studies [2,26–30,34]. It is notable that the response capabilities of the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 colloidal spheres (0–50 μM for KMnO4 and 0–250 μM for VC) are substantially larger than those reported for (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 nanowires (0–5 μM for KMnO4 and 0–10 μM for VC) [2].

In the presence of 25 μM MnO4 −, the 544 nm emission of the (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 colloidal suspension was monitored at different time intervals to test the response rate of the luminescence signal in the oxidation process (Fig. 9 , left part, red square). Immediate intensity decrease was observed and an equilibrium was reached at ˜18 s. Subsequent addition of 150 μM VC into the above luminescence quenched suspension induced a rapid luminescence recovery and an equilibrium was almost reached at ˜50 s (Fig. 9, right part, green square). The above results thus indicate that the redox of Ce3+/Ce4+ pair is quick and the monospheres have fairy good sensitivities toward both MnO4 − and VC.

Homogeneous precipitation yielded (Ce0.90Tb0.10)PO4 monospheres with an average crystallite size of ˜15.8 nm (via XRD) and average diameters of ˜1.39 ± 0.11 μm (via FE-SEM) and ˜347 nm (via BET). Luminescence quenching and recovery responses of the monospheres toward MnO4 − oxidant and vitamin C reductant were demonstrated in the wide ranges of 0–50 μM and 0–250 μM, respectively. The emission intensity (I) and MnO4 − concentration (C) can be expressed with the single exponentials of I = 0.02061 + 0.93435exp(-0.11073C) and I = 0.0225 + 0.94115exp(-0.09377C) for the 544 and 350 nm luminescence of Tb3+ and Ce3+, while the linear relations of I = -0.13 + 0.00459C and I = -0.23 + 0.005C may apply for the emission intensities of Tb3+ and Ce3+ and vitamin C concentration (C), respectively. The monospheres also exhibited the favorable response time of ˜18 s for 25 μM of MnO4- and ˜50 s for 150 μM of vitamin C.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51702020, and 51672039) and the Doctoral Research Fund of Liaoning Province (Grant No. 20170520103).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2019.04.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent sensing of colloidal CePO4:Tb nanorods for rapid, ultrasensitive and selective detection of vitamin C
Nanotechnology
